regions: Central and Eastern Europe, Middle East and Africa

dependencies: Four data centers located in the U.S., the United Kingdom and Singapore
Third-party suppliers of hardware and infrastructure required to operate data centers
Third-party service providers for processing services
Financial institution clients for transaction processing
Merchants and acquirers for acceptance network
risks: Supply chain disruptions such as manufacturing, shipping delays, and service disruption due to cyber attacks
Extended supply chain or service disruption could impact processing or delivery of technology services
Dependence on third-party service providers for various services associated with payments network
Failure of third parties to perform or deliver adequate services or comply with regulatory obligations
Third-party suppliers may not follow required contractual standards, may not be able to timely patch vulnerabilities or fix security defects
Data security, cybersecurity and operational resilience posture of acquired entities, joint ventures or companies invested in may not be adequate

regulatory_risks: Complex and evolving global regulations that could harm business and financial results
Interchange reimbursement fee regulations (U.S. Dodd-Frank Act limits debit interchange, EU IFR caps consumer credit and debit interchange)
Network exclusivity and routing restrictions (Dodd-Frank Act limits network exclusivity for debit/prepaid, IFR prohibits restrictions on multiple payment brands)
No-surcharge rules impacted by litigation, regulation and legislation
Privacy, data use, AI and cybersecurity regulations (GDPR in Europe, state privacy laws in U.S., China's Personal Information Protection Law, India's Personal Data Protection Act)
Supervisory oversight by Federal Banking Agencies (FBA), Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), central banks in various countries
European regulations: IFR, PSD2 (Payment Services Directive 2), strong customer authentication requirements
UK regulations: Designated as Recognized Payment System, regulated by Payment Systems Regulator (PSR)
Anti-corruption laws (U.S. FCPA, UK Bribery Act)
Anti-money laundering and anti-terrorist financing laws (U.S. Bank Secrecy Act)
Economic and trade sanctions (OFAC sanctions on Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Syria, Crimea, Donetsk and Luhansk regions)
Government-imposed market participation restrictions (China BCCI license requirements, India data localization mandate)
Interchange rate regulations in multiple jurisdictions (Australia RBA, New Zealand, UAE, Costa Rica, Turkey)
Cross-border interchange rate regulations (EU settlement with European Commission, Costa Rica, Turkey, UK PSR review)
Network fees regulatory interest (UK PSR market review, Australia, EU, Chile, Greece)
Competition law enforcement and antitrust regulations
Central bank oversight and designation as systemically important payment system (Brazil, India, UK, EU, Canada, Ukraine)
Licensing requirements for new products and capabilities (tokenization, push payments, new flows, Visa B2B Connect)
Payment institution and money transmitter regulations
Corporate responsibility and sustainability reporting requirements (EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive, SEC climate-related disclosures, California greenhouse gas emissions disclosure)
Open banking and open finance requirements (PSD2 in Europe, initiatives in Australia, Brazil, Canada, U.S.)
Personal financial data rights (CFPB final rule in U.S.)
Cyber incident reporting requirements across numerous jurisdictions with short timeframes
Pillar Two global minimum tax (15%) from OECD, expected to apply beginning fiscal 2025
Digital services taxes introduced by various countries
Tax law changes and OECD proposals for modernizing international tax system

sanction_risks: OFAC sanctions prevent entities domiciled in Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Syria, Crimea, Donetsk and Luhansk regions from issuing or acquiring Visa cards
Economic sanctions imposed on Russia by U.S., EU, UK and other jurisdictions in fiscal 2022
Suspended operations in Russia in March 2022, no longer generating revenue from domestic and cross-border activities related to Russia
War in Ukraine and further actions by or in response to Russia or its allies could adversely affect business
Geopolitical events, sanctions, trade tensions have intensified activities to restrict participation in processing regional transactions
Some countries expressing concerns about reliance on U.S. financial services companies after Russia sanctions
BRICS countries exploring ways to lessen dependence on Western payments systems
